text: The robotic surgical setup is shown with four 8mm robotic trocars positioned across the mid-abdomen and a 12mm assist port. The robot is approaching from the right side, and the specimen can be extracted through an extended supraumbilical port or a Pfannenstiel incision.
The surgeon begins the procedure by placing a DEMA stitch to retract the left lobe of the liver, providing clear access to the surgical field.

The DEMA stitch is visible, functioning as a hammock to retract the left lateral segment of the liver, which appears small in size. This technique effectively supports the liver without the need for external retractors.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac and uses a vessel sealer to mobilize the stomach. The splenic flexure becomes visible, followed by the division of the short gastric vessels using the vessel sealer.

The surgeon modifies the exposure to access the remaining short gastric vessels attached to the superior pole of the spleen. An intraoperative ultrasound is used to identify the pancreatic tumor and surrounding vasculature, aiding in the decision of the transection plane. The stomach is retracted to facilitate the mobilization of the infrapancreatic margin.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to mobilize tissue within an avascular plane, continuing the lateral dissection with visible precision.

The surgeon visualizes the distal transverse colon and splenic flexure, using hook cautery to dissect the splenocolic ligament until vessels are encountered. The vessel sealer is then employed for further dissection.

The surgeon targets the inferior pole of the spleen for mobilization, using the third robotic arm and assistant port to apply downward traction on the colon. The dissection continues until the splenocolic and splenodiaphragmatic attachments are divided. The next step involves dissecting the splenic artery.

The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to identify the location for dividing the splenic artery. A vessel loop is placed around the splenic artery for control, followed by division using a vascular stapler.

The surgeon completes the suprapancreatic margin mobilization from the transection area of the splenic artery to the superior pole of the spleen. Additional mobilization off the retroperitoneum is performed to encircle the pancreas fully. The tumor is visible, bulging off the posterior pancreas in the upper portion of the video.

The surgeon encircles the pancreas with umbilical tape to manipulate and orient it for division. The pancreas appears normal and thick, requiring two staple loads with a black stapler for complete division. Surgicel is applied to the pancreatic transection margin to control bleeding. The final retroperitoneal dissection is completed to free the specimen, which is placed in an endo-catch bag and extracted through an extended supraumbilical port site. A drain is then placed.
